title: Civilization I, II, III
whatItIs:
Civilization I, II, III is a simple way to think about civilizational scale. Civilization I is the agrarian, city-and-empire phase; Civilization II is the industrial, fossil-fuel, nation-state phase; Civilization III is the networked, planetary-intelligence phase that is still forming.

The point is not to force history into a rigid ladder. The point is to notice that each stage changes energy use, information flow, coordination scale, and the kind of power that matters most.
whyItMatters:
This topic matters because it helps you see why some institutions feel modern while others feel ancient, even when they coexist. It also helps explain why AI, biotech, space infrastructure, and global communications feel like more than just new tools.

The lens is useful for asking what kind of civilisation we are building next, what bottlenecks remain, and what could derail the transition.
keyIdeas: Agriculture makes settlement and hierarchy possible
Civilisational stages overlap rather than replace each other cleanly
Power follows the dominant infrastructure of the era
frontier: The frontier question is whether a planetary civilisation can become intelligent enough to manage its own risks without collapsing under its own complexity. AI, climate systems, and global interdependence make that question concrete rather than theoretical.
openQuestions: What actually defines a civilisational stage?
Are we already in a transition rather than a new era?
Can planetary coordination scale fast enough to match planetary risk?
